name: The Heights
audience: Young professionals, foodies, and urban-minded buyers who want a walkable neighborhood with real personality and the best restaurant scene in central Arkansas.
pain_points: Limited inventory — desirable homes move fast and attract multiple offers
Older housing stock means higher maintenance and renovation costs
Parking is genuinely frustrating on Kavanaugh on weekends
Premium price for the zip code relative to square footage
what_draws_people: Kavanaugh Blvd — a walkable main street with restaurants, coffee shops, and bars
Highest concentration of locally-owned restaurants in central Arkansas
Historic bungalows and Craftsman homes with real character
Strong sense of neighborhood identity and community events
Short distance to everything — downtown, UAMS, Midtown

typical_weekend: Saturday starts with coffee at Onyx and a walk down Kavanaugh. Lunch at Raduno or Cache, then browsing the independent shops on Hillcrest. Sunday brunch at ZaZa or the Root Cafe, followed by a run on the River Trail.

name: West Little Rock
audience: Families and professionals who want suburban convenience, good schools, and easy access to retail without sacrificing proximity to the city.
pain_points: Car-dependent — almost nothing is walkable
Generic suburban feel; lacks neighborhood identity compared to Heights or SoMa
Traffic on Chenal Pkwy during peak hours is genuinely bad
Lots of new development but not all of it is quality construction
what_draws_people: Excellent access to retail — Kroger Marketplace, Target, Whole Foods all within a short drive
Good school zones, including Hall High and Pleasant Valley Elementary
Newer construction and larger lot sizes
Relatively short drive to both downtown and Chenal Valley
Strong resale market and consistent appreciation

name: Downtown Little Rock
audience: Young professionals, urban enthusiasts, and buyers who want walkable city living with river access and a growing food and arts scene.
pain_points: Fewer family-friendly amenities and school options than suburban neighborhoods
Parking can be challenging and often paid
Condo HOA fees add to monthly housing costs
The urban revival is real but still uneven block-to-block
what_draws_people: River Market District — Arkansas's best urban food and entertainment hub
Big Dam Bridge and River Trail access steps from your door
Lofts and condos with genuine character in historic buildings
First Thursday art walks and year-round events
Proximity to state government, law firms, and financial institutions

name: Maumelle
audience: Families and professionals who want a planned, safe community north of the river with lake access and a strong sense of neighborhood without the big-city noise.
pain_points: The river crossing — I-40 bridge traffic during rush hour is significant
Limited dining and entertainment within the community itself
Feels isolated from Little Rock's urban energy
Older planned-community housing stock from the 1980s-90s is dated
what_draws_people: Planned community design with parks, trails, and green space baked in
Lake Maumelle access and water recreation
Lower crime rates and strong neighborhood watch culture
Saline River Greenway trail system
Generally lower price per square foot than comparable LR neighborhoods

name: Cammack Village
audience: Buyers who want the Heights lifestyle — walkability, character, mature trees — but who place the highest priority on school quality and neighborhood prestige.
pain_points: Extremely limited inventory — only about 500 homes in the entire village
When something comes on the market, expect competition
No real commercial strip of its own; relies on the Heights for dining
Higher price premium than surrounding areas for the school zone benefit
what_draws_people: Surrounded on all sides by Little Rock but governed separately — its own police and services
Access to Pulaski Academy and Episcopal Collegiate feeder schools
Mature canopy and beautiful tree-lined streets
Strong community identity despite tiny size
Historic and architecturally distinctive homes

name: North Little Rock
audience: Value-oriented buyers, artists and creatives, and first-time buyers who want proximity to downtown without downtown prices, plus outdoor enthusiasts who live for Burns Park and the River Trail.

School quality is inconsistent across the district
Some corridors are genuinely rough; neighborhood research matters more here
Commute to Little Rock requires crossing the river
what_draws_people: Argenta Arts District — one of the most authentic arts neighborhoods in Arkansas
Burns Park — one of the largest municipal parks in the entire United States
Affordable entry prices with genuine upside
River Trail access and Junction Bridge to downtown LR

name: Conway

University of Central Arkansas (UCA) campus energy and cultural programming

name: Bryant
audience: Families who want the combination of an excellent school district, affordable new construction, and a south-side location that avoids north I-30 traffic.
pain_points: I-30 southbound traffic during peak hours can be brutal
Still relatively limited in terms of dining and entertainment
The suburban character is heavy — not for buyers who want city energy
Rapid growth means some growing pains in infrastructure
what_draws_people: Bryant School District — consistently top-10 in Arkansas, exceptional athletics
New construction at prices well below comparable LR neighborhoods
Saline County tax rates lower than Pulaski County
Strong community identity built around Hornet pride
Accessible to Little Rock without the city tax burden

name: Benton
audience: Value buyers, small-town lifestyle seekers, and investors who want Saline County tax advantages, community character, and a genuine small-town feel 20 minutes from Little Rock.
pain_points: More limited job market than Little Rock — most residents commute
Downtown Benton revitalization is early-stage
Limited dining and nightlife options
Older housing stock in many established neighborhoods
what_draws_people: Saline County seat with genuine small-town community culture
Lower real estate prices than Bryant with similar county tax advantages
Railroad Avenue revitalization bringing new energy to downtown
Tyndall Park and outdoor recreation options
Strong value play — lowest price-to-quality in the south metro

name: Hot Springs Village
audience: Retirees and pre-retirees seeking the best active adult lifestyle in Arkansas — multiple golf courses, beautiful lakes, low maintenance, and a tight-knit community of like-minded people.
pain_points: 45-60 minutes from Little Rock limits access to city amenities
The POA (Property Owners Association) fee is significant
Limited healthcare options within the village itself
The community skews older — not ideal for families or young buyers
Resale can be slower due to specialized buyer pool
what_draws_people: Largest gated community in the United States — 26,000+ acres
9 golf courses including Balboa and Coronado clubs
11 lakes within the village boundaries
Lock-and-leave lifestyle with extensive amenity infrastructure
Genuine community — the Village has its own newspaper, restaurants, and civic life

name: SoMa District
audience: Creatives, young professionals, and urban buyers who want the Heights' energy at a lower price point, with genuine arts culture, walkability, and a neighborhood that's still becoming something.
pain_points: Uneven development — some blocks are polished, others aren't yet
Limited family-oriented amenities
Parking can be scarce near South Main during events
School options require research and often private school consideration
what_draws_people: South on Main — one of Arkansas's most acclaimed restaurants
Lost Forty Brewing — the anchor of SoMa's social scene
South Main Creative Corridor — galleries, studios, and the Argenta-style arts energy
Genuinely walkable main street feeling on South Main
Lower buy-in than the Heights with similar appreciation trajectory

name: Riverdale
audience: Established professionals, empty nesters, and buyers who want proximity to the Heights and the river without the Heights price, in a quieter, more residential setting.
pain_points: Limited commercial corridor of its own — relies on the Heights for dining
Some of the housing stock is dated and requires investment
River Road traffic during peak hours
Less neighborhood identity than the Heights or SoMa
what_draws_people: Riverfront Park access and Big Dam Bridge proximity
Quieter, more residential character than the Heights proper
Older established homes with good bones and mature trees
Central location — everything is 10-15 minutes away
Generally lower prices than the Heights for comparable quality

name: Midtown Little Rock
audience: Value-seeking urban buyers, renters ready to buy, and investors who want Heights-adjacent character at first-time buyer prices, accepting that the neighborhood is still transitioning.
pain_points: Block-by-block quality variation — research specific streets carefully
Still genuinely transitional in some corridors
Less established commercial scene than the Heights
Some older housing stock requires significant renovation budget
what_draws_people: Heights-adjacent pricing — similar character at 20-30% lower cost
Markham Street corridor has genuine small-business energy developing
Historic housing stock with renovation potential
Increasingly attracting young creatives and first-time buyers
Strong long-term appreciation potential as the Heights prices out buyers

name: Scott / Landmark
audience: Buyers seeking land and acreage within metro distance, rural character, agricultural heritage, and significantly lower prices — often investors, farmers, or buyers who explicitly want space.
pain_points: Most remote from Little Rock amenities — not for the car-averse
Limited services, dining, and retail within the community
Older and more rural housing stock
Schools are limited options compared to most of the metro
what_draws_people: Land and acreage available at very accessible prices
Rural, agricultural character — horses, chickens, and space are all common
Historic Scott community with genuine small-town history
Very low price per acre compared to any other metro-adjacent area
Privacy, quiet, and genuine escape from suburban density

typical_weekend: Saturday morning feeding livestock or working the property. Drive to town for supplies. Sunday is community church — Scott has deep church community roots. Very self-sufficient, very quiet.
